perturbation.original_text: 2.2 "END USER CUSTOMER SERVICES" means the collective reference to warranty and post warranty (maintenance) services, including standard and enhanced warranty services, made available by McDATA and provided directly to End User Customers.
perturbation.changed_text: 2.2 "END USER CUSTOMER SERVICES" means services, including standard and enhanced warranty services, made available by McDATA and provided directly to End User Customers. Whether these services include warranty and post-warranty (maintenance) is at McDATA's discretion.
perturbation.explanation: By stating that the inclusion of warranty and post-warranty services is at McDATA's discretion, the definition contradicts the typical and legally understood meaning of 'End User Customer Services,' which generally encompasses such warranty services.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Magnuson-Moss Warranty Act sets requirements for warranties on consumer products. By making warranty services discretionary, the modified text could be interpreted as an attempt to circumvent the requirements for clear and conspicuous warranty terms, potentially violating the Act if the products covered are considered consumer products.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 15 U.S. Code § 2301 - Definitions | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 15 U.S. Code § 2301 - Definitions U.S. Code Notes Authorities (CFR) prev | next For the purposes of this chapter: (1) The term “ consumer product ” means any tangible personal property which is distributed in commerce and which is normally used for personal, family, or household purposes (including any such property intended to be attached to or installed in any real property without regard to whether it is so attached or installed). (2) The term “ Commission ” means the Federal Trade Commission . (3) The term “ consumer ” means a buyer (other than for purposes of resale) of any consumer product , any person to whom such product is transferred during the duration of an implied or written warranty (or service contract) applicable to the product, and any other person who is entitled by the terms of such warranty (or service contract) or under applicable State law to enforce against the warrantor (or service contractor) the obligations of the warranty (or service contract) . (4) The term “ supplier ” means any person engaged in the business of making a consumer product directly or indirectly available to consumers. (5) The term “ warrantor ” means any supplier or other person who gives or offers to give a written warranty or who is or may be obligated under an implied warranty . (6) The term “ written warranty ” means— (A) any written affirmation of fact or written promise made in connection with the sale of a consumer product by a supplier to a buyer which relates to the nature of the material or workmanship and affirms or promises that such material or workmanship is defect free or will meet a specified level of performance over a specified period of time, or (B) any undertaking in writing in connection with the sale by a supplier of a consumer product to refund, repair, replace, or take other remedial act
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a contradiction by making warranty services discretionary. The original text defines 'END USER CUSTOMER SERVICES' to include warranty and post-warranty services. The modified text allows McDATA to decide whether to include these services, which goes against the typical understanding. Scraped Law Snippet 1, under 15 U.S. Code § 2301, defines key terms like 'consumer product,' 'supplier,' and 'warrantor,' highlighting the legal importance of warranties. By making warranty services optional, the modified text undermines the protection that the Magnuson-Moss Warranty Act aims to provide to consumers for consumer products, which creates a legal contradiction because it deviates from the legally recognized importance and definition of warranties associated with end-user customer services.

perturbation.original_text: 10.4 THE WARRANTIES, IF ANY, PROVIDED IN THIS AGREEMENT (INCLUDING THE APPENDICES AND CHANNEL NOTIFICATION DOCUMENTS) OR AS PROVIDED WITH THE PRODUCTS ARE GIVEN IN LIEU OF ALL OTHER McDATA Corporation Standard Terms of Reseller Agreement June 2004 4 WARRANTIES, WHETHER EXPRESS OR IMPLIED, INCLUDING ANY IMPLIED WARRANTY OF MERCHANTABILITY, NONINFRINGEMENT OR FITNESS FOR A PARTICULAR PURPOSE, ALL OF WHICH ARE HEREBY EXPRESSLY DISCLAIMED AND EXCLUDED.
perturbation.changed_text: 10.4 THE WARRANTIES, IF ANY, PROVIDED IN THIS AGREEMENT (INCLUDING THE APPENDICES AND CHANNEL NOTIFICATION DOCUMENTS) OR AS PROVIDED WITH THE PRODUCTS ARE GIVEN IN LIEU OF ALL OTHER WARRANTIES, WHETHER EXPRESS OR IMPLIED, ALL OF WHICH ARE HEREBY EXPRESSLY DISCLAIMED AND EXCLUDED. Any warranty of merchantability, NONINFRINGEMENT OR fitness for a particular purpose is expressly included and not disclaimed.
perturbation.explanation: This creates a contradiction: The first sentence disclaims all warranties including merchantability, while the second sentence states that the warranty of merchantability is expressly included. This internal contradiction makes the warranty terms unenforceable due to uncertainty.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 2-316 governs the exclusion or modification of warranties. To be effective, disclaimers of the implied warranty of merchantability must be conspicuous and use specific language. The contradictory language here creates ambiguity, which under the UCC is generally construed against the drafter (McDATA) and may render the disclaimer ineffective, thus failing to properly disclaim the implied warranty of merchantability under the UCC.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-316. Exclusion or Modification of Warranties. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-316. Exclusion or Modification of Warranties. (1) Words or conduct relevant to the creation of an express warranty and words or conduct tending to negate or limit warranty shall be construed wherever reasonable as consistent with each other; but subject to the provisions of this Article on parol or extrinsic evidence (Section 2-202 ) negation or limitation is inoperative to the extent that such construction is unreasonable. (2)Subject to subsection (3), to exclude or modify the implied warranty of merchantability or any part of it the language must mention merchantability and in case of a writing must be conspicuous, and to exclude or modify any implied warranty of fitness the exclusion must be by a writing and conspicuous. Language to exclude all implied warranties of fitness is sufficient if it states, for example, that "There are no warranties which extend beyond the description on the face hereof." (3)Notwithstanding subsection (2) (a) unless the circumstances indicate otherwise, all implied warranties are excluded by expressions like "as is", "with all faults" or other language which in common understanding calls the buyer's attention to the exclusion of warranties and makes plain that there is no implied warranty; and (b) when the buyer before entering into the contract has examined the goods or the sample or model as fully as he desired or has refused to examine the goods there is no implied warranty with regard to defects which an examination ought in the circumstances to have revealed to him; and (c) an implied warranty can also be excluded or modified by course of dealing or course of performance or usage of trade. (4) Remedies for breach of warranty can be limited in accordance with the provisions of this Article on liquidation or limitation of damages and on contractual modification of remed
perturbation.justification: The changed text creates a contradiction by first disclaiming all warranties and then expressly including the warranty of merchantability, NONINFRINGEMENT, or fitness for a particular purpose, which undermines the effectiveness of the disclaimer. UCC § 2-316, as provided in Scraped Law Snippet 1, states that to exclude or modify the implied warranty of merchantability, the language must mention merchantability and be conspicuous. The original text attempted to disclaim this warranty, but the changed text explicitly includes it, creating ambiguity and negating the initial disclaimer. This contradiction would likely be construed against the drafter, rendering the disclaimer ineffective and thus contradicting the requirements for a valid disclaimer under UCC § 2-316.

perturbation.original_text: 12.6 GOVERNING LAWS. This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Colorado excluding its choice of law provisions. The United Nations Convention on Contracts for the International Sale of Goods (CISG) is specifically excluded and shall not be applicable to any transaction contemplated herein.
perturbation.changed_text: 12.6 GOVERNING LAWS. This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Colorado including its choice of law provisions. The United Nations Convention on Contracts for the International Sale of Goods (CISG) is specifically included and shall be applicable to any transaction contemplated herein.
perturbation.explanation: The original text explicitly excludes the CISG. The modified text includes it, creating a direct contradiction.
perturbation.contradicted_law: United Nations Convention on Contracts for the International Sale of Goods (CISG)
perturbation.law_explanation: By including the CISG, the contract now falls under its jurisdiction, which dictates the international sale of goods. This directly contradicts the original intent of the agreement and subjects the agreement to a different legal framework. The application of the CISG would alter the interpretation of contract terms and potentially impose different obligations on the parties involved, especially if the agreement involves parties from different nations that have adopted the CISG.
perturbation.justification: The original contract specifically excludes the CISG, while the changed text explicitly includes it. This is a direct contradiction because the original text and the changed text have completely opposite legal effects regarding the CISG's applicability. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation confirm this creates a misalignment of terminology that leads to a legal contradiction, as defined in the prompt.
